sentences.text: Well, this was the liquidation of the ghetto.
The trains for waiting for us.
We did not escape from the ghetto.
No.
We had to--
The journey took us I think three-- two nights, two days and two nights.
It was August-- August the 24th that we left the Lodz ghetto.
And we arrived about the 26th or the 27th of August, 1944.
Liquidation.
It's coincidental now that it's almost 50 years, a few days before the 50 years, that we left the Lodz ghetto.
And as I came in I saw my mother had really long hair.

She had a braid, and-- and my little sister, who had blue eyes and blonde hair, blonde braids, I took a scissor and I cut my sister's braids off because I thought maybe with shorter hair they-- they-- they won't shave our heads.
So I cut my mother's braid and my sister's two braids, and I put it in-- in my little sack that I have.
But when we came to-- to the segregation, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my-- my sister was taken.
We were taken on the right side, first my younger sister, Regina, then my sister, Ruth.
My mother was on the right-hand side I came on the right-hand side.
And when my little sister was taken, just pulled away from us, my mother grabbed her hand and went with her on the left-hand side.
I started crying and screaming.
There was a young soldier escorting us and he asked me, [SPEAKING GERMAN], like a dog is barking, why do you cry like that?
I said, they just took my mother and my sister away.
He says, a mother?
Who has a mother?
I says, well, why shouldn't I have a mother?
I still did not-- I did not picture the-- that cruelty of Germans at the time.
I don't think I was smart enough or visualized what was going on.
And he said, well, I'm-- I'm young, and I don't have a mother.
And I says, that doesn't mean that I'm not supposed to have one.
Then he turned around and he says, don't worry.
Your mother is going to go to work to a match factory.
And when we came to Auschwitz, and I saw what's going on, they-- they undressed us completely.
We had to undress, we had to put all our belonging and undress completely naked in front of people.
And then they told us to go in into like a shower room.
And I said to myself, am I happy that my mother does not have to go through the cruelty that men are walking back and forth and looking at our naked bodies?
You thought she was being spared that?
Yes.
I thought that she was sent to a factory, and we are just being here in no man's land, in some-- in some horrible, horrible place.
I did not know.
I did not understand what that match factory meant till after the war.
I kept on telling everybody, my mother is in a match factory.
She works in a match factory.
I could not believe what they meant with that.
After the war, I found out what he meant by the match factory.
Nine years old.
Lea, [?
Lilke. ?] There that is a plaque in the [INAUDIBLE] Memorial in her name.
My mother thought that we are old enough to take care on ourselves.
[CRYING]
No.
I had this feeling.
When she left us and went with our little sister-- [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] she knew she wouldn't be able to-- to live all by herself, she was too young.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm just very sad that my mother didn't go with us.
She could have lived probably another few years.
But then this was her-- her way of showing that she loved us, and she loved my little sister.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
Well, I--
I think that I took the blame for them not coming with us because later as I looked at it, I said, if I would not have cut it-- maybe Samson had his hair cut off, and I took the power away from them.
[CRYING]
I know that.
I'm trying to tell myself many-- many sleepless nights that I had-- that I tried my best at the time, that what I understood to do.
If I would not have do it, maybe-- maybe they would shave their heads.
They shaved them anyway, but we-- we did not know at that time.
I just wanted to save the shame of being without hair.
For a woman, it's a very, very drastic thing to-- to bear.

Because when-- when we were in Auschwitz, and we were staying outside and was a group of women, and one of the girl-- of the girls that walked with me in the ghetto said, I hear Sally's voice but I don't know where she is.
She's just standing right there looking in my face, but she did not recognize me.
And when I went out once from a barrack, and there was a little window, passing by was like a dark little corridor, and there was a window.
And somehow I turned my head and I saw my face.
I turned around to see if somebody is in back with me that looks a little bit like me.
I did not recognize myself without hair because it's-- it's a terrible thing for a person to know that.
And we stayed in Auschwitz, which was a very, very drastic experience.
We were laying on the ground and the first-- first confrontation, they gave us a huge pot about that big with some soup in it with no spoons.
And I was sitting the first and there were four-- my sisters-- my two sisters, Regina and Ruth and two other girls were in back of them.
And when they gave me the pot and a girl-- the Jewish girl, [? none ?] of the blockalteste, or whatever-- I asked if I could have a spoon and she started laughing hysterically like a hyena.
I could not understand.
I says, why are you laughing?
She says, you are here just a few hours, and you want a spoon already?
So they told me to tilt the-- the pot and drink from it.
I was very hungry.
We didn't eat for two days and two nights.
But I could not eat like that so I gave it back to my sisters and somehow they ate it-- I don't know, with their hand or whatever.
But then we were changed to another barrack, and again we were laying on the ground.
But you were able to stay together, you and your sisters?
Yeah.
And-- well, a lot of people did not know that we were sisters.
We kept a secret--
Yes.
Yes, we did.
Well, we heard-- we heard in Auschwitz that Mengele who's the doctor that made the separations, that he separates twins and sisters because one should not keep up the spirit of the other.
If you don't have anybody, he said, you probably will die faster.
No, but some-- we were told that.
That-- So when we found this out and we were transferred later to a barrack where there were younger children, and it was close to the kitchen.
And my both sisters went out, and there was a garbage can with potato peels.
And we were very hungry.
So they got some potato peels and somebody noticed them.
So they called them, they told them, and the road was of stones, very pointy stones that, you know, your feet when you had no shoes.

But they called [INAUDIBLE], who was the highest official of Auschwitz, and she made them kneel down on these stones and then she took both heads of my sisters and just knocked them both together in-- in-- in a force and she told them to run back to the barracks.
And they wouldn't-- she wouldn't-- they would not-- she wouldn't give them even the peels anymore.
They were just-- they came, they-- they could not see straight because, you know, sparks were in front of their eyes from that impact of both heads.
But later we found-- we found out that we are going on a transport and they took us out.
And the nights were terrible.
We were laying there, and if you had to go to release yourself, there was a pail outside in the back.
And there were electric wires in the back.
And there were a few times, and this is not making up stories.
This is staying in front of my eyes.
Every time I went out and I looked at the-- at the wires, they talked to me.
They said, come here.
Come here.
And I said to myself, if I come there, and my sisters are going to take the same example, and nobody will be left from the whole family.
I cannot go to the wires.
And I went back into the barrack and-- and laid down, and I could not sleep.
And this repeated a few times.
Yes.
I-- I-- Subconsciously I wanted to end it.
I really did not want to end it.
I did not want to leave my sisters.
I said once to myself in a dream, I says, how happy will I be when after the war I can show my parents that we saved ourselves for them?
[CRYING]
Yeah.
We were taken that time from that barrack to go on a transport.
I was 16; my sister, Regina, was 14; and my sister Ruth was 12.
I was from the three of us the oldest, yes.
I had older sisters that were not with us anymore, but I was the oldest one in the three of us.
Yes, I did feel an obligation.
And we was taken to--
Yes.
Thank God, we are all together here in Los Angeles.
And we also have a sister that came back.
We lost one sister in Russia-- who-- who died of typhoid fever with a child and one came back.
She married my brother in Russia and came back, and we brought her from Israel.
They lived in Israel and we brought her here, and she lives now with us together.
No, no.
She had a child and a husband.
Her husband lives now-- I don't know if he's still alive, but he immigrated to-- to Israel.
He lost a limb in-- in the Russian Army and then after the liberation, he decided to go to Israel.
The child's name I don't know.
But her name was Esther and he was Moses.
I have a picture of them here because my-- my other sister from-- received it, saw him after the war.
So he gave her a picture before you went to-- before he went to Israel, he gave her a picture.
I don't remember.
I have to ask my-- my sister?
He lived in Lodz.
In Israel, in Tel Aviv.
In Russia, they were around Siberia, in Siberia someplace.
And-- so--
Yeah, I just wanted-- Yeah.
This was block number three that we had a selection.
And I went out the first, but there was an opening between.
And I wanted to jump on the other side.
And there were the girls that were in charge, the Sturmbannfuhrer.
They just kept on pushing me back.
And I constantly wanted to jump.
And I said to my sisters, after I'm on this side, you jump through.
But they somehow kept on pushing me back on this side.
So it came my turn to go out from the barrack in front of Mengele, and there were some nurses there.

And when I went out, we made up that after I go, they will wait till other-- till other girls will go out and then the-- the second sister would go out and then again they would-- she would wait and then go out after that.
So when I came out, and I stood outside-- because he turned you around and looked over and told me to go in the-- I think was the right hand side-- and I-- Do you remember him?
Do you remember his face?
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
I remember his dark eyes.
And I said to myself, if-- if he would stay in a line, the Germans would gladly take him out as a Jew because he had a very dark complexion and dark hair and brown eyes.

And I remember distinctly the-- the thought of it because my little sister, when she went to the line to get some butter, when we were sitting in Lodz before the ghetto, she was always taken out-- the German soldiers used take her out from the line and say, you little girl, you don't have to stay line, you are an Aryan.
And they used to lead her into the room to get whatever, you know, the ration was that time.
So in a-- when I looked at him, I says, he looks probably more Jewish than I do-- of course, not the nose, apparently.
But I waited and waited and my eyes [INAUDIBLE] went out till my second sister came out and we hugged and kissed.

And then the third one, Ruth, that lives over here, it took her a long-- an awful long time to come out, and we thought already, God forbid if she's not on the other side or she jumped over or whatever.
We knew-- we knew that they were sending out from Auschwitz.
We did not know where.
And we also did not know which side goes where.
That's why, you know, if we-- I would have jumped on the other side, I would have been in a crematorium.
But they took us out, and then my third sister came, and we hugged and kissed.
And they told us to go.
They-- they formed-- they put us all together and they took us to another camp.
We were on the C lager.
They took us to A lager.
And we were there I think two nights-- to nights and-- two nights and two days.
And they brought in a group of Hungarian girls.
They gave us blankets to cover up.
It was cold already.
It was like November or October, I think.
And the girls came-- the girls that came after us, they took the blankets, and they tore them apart and made themselves wrappings or whatever.
When the [GERMAN] saw that, they took them outside.
And my sister was told to them-- a woman came in that worked in that A lager, and she called a name.
And I said to her, I worked with your father.
So she became very befriended with us, because I gave her regards from her father, who was an older man.
And she called my younger sister on the side and said, Ruth, if they take you out at night kick, scream, bite, do anything you can, but don't stay.
See that you can escape because when they take out, she says, at night, it goes directly to the crematorium.
And here they're taking us out-- out at night and she got so hysterical she cried and she-- Ruth, what happened?
She would not tell us what happened.
She cried so bitterly, so that she really got hysterical.
And they took the other group away, and they told-- they kept us maybe half a night in the cold outside.
And after that, they told us to go back in.
But the other-- the other group they took away and we never heard from them since.
And then they put us on trains.
The next day, I went to get water.
They needed somebody to bring water, and I was the first one so I can wash my face.
And my sisters came running, saying, the train is going already away.
They're taking us away from Auschwitz.
From Auschwitz, we are going to a-- to a [INAUDIBLE] camp, to a working place.
So I-- I would not give up the pail of water.
I ran with the pail of water, spilling half of it so to have a little bit of water.
And there was a girl with us.
They took her to another train where-- and she was all alone.
So I went over to German, and I said-- you know, I speak German-- so I said to him, there's a girl here that is all alone, and can we exchange somebody else and have her with us?
And we-- you know, we adopted her as the forth sister and she was with us, you know, till we were liberated and we came to-- to Landsberg [?
Foundation ?] and she left.
So the guard allowed it?
Yeah.
They allowed it because I said that she's like-- she's all alone, and she's young.
And she's a good worker.
And so to them, it didn't make any difference.
You know, there were-- there were [INAUDIBLE] them.
I tell you in a little while when we were on the train what happened when we-- we boarded the train, and we had an older man who was the-- the-- like the director of the [INAUDIBLE].
But in our compartment was a young soldier, and he was sitting on the bench, and one-- the first day he ask who went to gymnasium.
This was like here high school or maybe a little higher education.
And there was a my-- a teacher of mine.
And she was a physical education teacher, but she was a teacher, she has education.
So she says, I went to gymnasium.
He gave her a problem, an algebra problem, and she didn't know what-- how to do it.
So he asked again who-- I don't know if he wanted to find out if we come from a forest like we pretended that we are-- oh, we are really human beings that went to school one day or not.
So he asked again if somebody else went and then a few people, they couldn't make it.
And I was standing there and I said, could I see that?
And he said to me, well, you didn't go to gymnasium, you're too young.
I said, no, but can I just see it?
And I figured it out not the way he wanted, you know, but the way I felt that I-- and I figured it out and he looked at that and he says, you are right.
How did you figure this out?
And I says, in my own way, I figured it out.
He was sitting on a bench.
He says, you come here, and you sit down on the bench.
You don't have to sit on the floor.
He brought a blanket and put over my shoulders the blanket, and he was like a friend and a young fellow.
He had completely his face cut up.
He must have been on the-- on the war zone front and was very much injured.
So instead to send him back, they gave him a job, you know, to commute people back and forth from Auschwitz to the camps.
And all of a sudden, it got very noisy in the compartment and the girls started fighting, and he grabbed his rifle and went at the girls.
And I says, hold it.
And he looked at me.
I says, would you kill them, I said.
He said, yes.
I said, why would you kill them?
What did they do?
He says, [SPEAKING GERMAN], this is a-- a order.
I says, there's nobody here to give you the order.
He said, but they are making noise.
There may be a rebel or something.
I says, would you kill your father?
And he said, yes.
And I says, would you kill your mother, who carried you under her heart?
He turned around and cried.
And I have witnesses.
And he never said a word.

When the train stopped, he went down with a train with tomatoes and onions and he took off his hat, which was against the law to take a soldier's hat and put in tomatoes and onions, and brought it to the compartment for us to eat.
So in a way, they were monsters, but somehow you had to touch a soft spot in their body to see if they were still human.
And that boy was still human.
Although he was killing people.
He did not mind to take the gun and kill 60 girls laying around.
He would kill his father, but he could not kill his mother.
Is that what you were trying to do, touch something human in him?
Yes.
I think I wanted to see if still any-- any spark of humanity remained in-- in him because he was a bitter-- a bitter young man.
Maybe because of the wounds that he had or maybe of the-- what he saw during-- in the war, or maybe-- who knows what it was.
But I could see the bitterness in him, and I was maybe younger than he was.
Because he was of military age.
I don't know if they took him at 16 or 18, but he was very, very young.
He never touched-- till we arrived in Auschwitz, he never touched anybody.
I think I did not realize.
I-- I did not realize what was waiting me, what I-- what I can-- you know, what I can be punished with, what I can confront.
I want a child.
At the moment, I was a child.
I did not realize what I'm doing.
But I knew that I can bring.
When he turned around, and I saw tears in his eyes, I knew that there is still something left in him.
And then we had arrived.
We came to Leipzig.
Yes.
Yes.
At one time we were in the barracks, and the girls were very impatient.
Either hungry-- we were hungry-- well, we were hungry all the time.
But there was no room and one was pushing the other, and there was a little noise and there was one-- I don't know, she said she was from Czechoslovakia.
I don't know if she was Romanian or Hungarian, I'm sure Czechoslovakia.
But she was one of the [GERMAN], and she wore the most gorgeous Chinese robe silk robe and her hair was put up in curlers, and she had a big stick in her hand.
And she was going and knocking.
She said, [SPEAKING CZECH].
I stepped over dead bodies and if you're not going to be quiet, I'm going to-- to kill you all.
No, that's Czech.
That's Czech.
[SPEAKING CZECH]
